text: Aldehydes and ketones are distinguished by Tollens' reagent (ammoniacal AgNO₃). Aldehydes give:
explanation: Tollens' test: RCHO + 2[Ag(NH₃)₂]⁺ + 2OH⁻ → RCOOH + 2Ag↓ + 4NH₃. Silver mirror formed. Ketones give no reaction.
name: Tollens' Test

explanation: Fehling's test: gives brick-red Cu₂O precipitate with aliphatic aldehydes. Aromatic aldehydes (benzaldehyde) do NOT reduce Fehling's. Ketones: negative.
name: Fehling's Test

options: An aldehyde/ketone with α-hydrogen reacting with another carbonyl compound in base
explanation: Aldol condensation: base-catalysed reaction between two carbonyl compounds with α-H. Product is β-hydroxy aldehyde/ketone (aldol).
name: Aldol Condensation

explanation: Cannizzaro: aldehydes lacking α-H (HCHO, PhCHO, CCl₃CHO) undergo disproportionation in conc. NaOH → one mole oxidised to acid, one reduced to alcohol.
name: Cannizzaro Reaction

text: The acidity of carboxylic acids compared to alcohols is due to:
explanation: RCOOH (pKa ~5) >> ROH (pKa ~16). The carboxylate anion –COO⁻ is resonance-stabilised (negative charge delocalised over 2 oxygens).
name: Acidity of Carboxylic Acids

text: Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky (HVZ) reaction introduces halogen at _____ position of carboxylic acids:
explanation: HVZ reaction: RCOOH + X₂ (PCl₃/PBr₃ catalyst) → α-halocarboxylic acid. X introduced at α-carbon of the acid.
name: HVZ Reaction

text: Reduction of carboxylic acids to primary alcohols requires:
explanation: RCOOH → RCH₂OH requires LiAlH₄ (strong reducing agent). NaBH₄ does NOT reduce COOH group. LiAlH₄ reduces COOH to 1° alcohol.
name: Reduction of Carboxylic Acid

text: The reaction of aldehyde with ammonia derivatives (NH₂–G) giving C=N compounds is:
explanation: R–CHO + H₂N–G → R–CH=N–G + H₂O. Two steps: (1) nucleophilic addition to give carbinolamine, (2) dehydration. Net: condensation.
name: Nucleophilic Addition-Elimination

explanation: Clemmensen reduction: R–CO–R' + Zn(Hg)/conc. HCl → R–CH₂–R'. Reduces C=O to CH₂ (removes oxygen). For acid-tolerant substrates.
name: Clemmensen Reduction

text: Wolff-Kishner reduction converts ketone to alkane using:
explanation: Wolff-Kishner: R₂C=O + NH₂NH₂ → R₂C=NNH₂ (hydrazone), then KOH/Δ → R₂CH₂ + N₂. Base conditions (complementary to Clemmensen).
name: Wolff-Kishner Reduction

text: Acetic acid (CH₃COOH) reacts with NH₃ followed by heating to give:
explanation: CH₃COOH + NH₃ → CH₃COONH₄ (ammonium salt) → heat → CH₃CONH₂ (acetamide) + H₂O.
name: Acid to Amide

text: The Baeyer-Villiger oxidation of a ketone with a peracid gives:
options: Ester (inserts oxygen into C–C bond next to carbonyl)
explanation: Baeyer-Villiger: R–CO–R' + peracid (mCPBA) → R–COO–R' (ester). An oxygen atom is inserted between carbonyl C and one alkyl group.
name: Baeyer-Villiger Oxidation

text: Benzaldehyde (PhCHO) is distinguished from acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO) by:
options: Fehling's test (PhCHO does not reduce Fehling's, CH₃CHO does)
explanation: Fehling's test: CH₃CHO → brick-red Cu₂O (positive). PhCHO → no reaction (negative). Both give positive Tollens' test.
name: Distinguishing Aldehydes

text: The boiling point of carboxylic acids is higher than alcohols of similar molecular weight because:
options: They form dimers via double hydrogen bonding
explanation: Carboxylic acids form cyclic dimers via double O–H···O=C H-bonds. This dimerisation requires extra energy to break → very high boiling points.
name: High BP of Carboxylic Acids

text: The reaction of HCHO (formaldehyde) with excess NaOH is:
explanation: HCHO has no α-H → Cannizzaro: 2HCHO + NaOH → CH₃OH (methanol) + HCOONa (sodium formate). Cross-Cannizzaro also possible.
name: HCHO Cannizzaro
